evidence: Sovereign AI initiatives… could limit our ability to deploy centralized AI tools globally and may require significant investment in region-specific infrastructure.
our increasing use of AI tools and technologies, including generative AI, introduces new security risks
Chief AI Technology Officer ('CATO')… cybersecurity sub-committee… reviews our cybersecurity risk assessments
We may not efficiently and effectively implement and leverage technological advancements such as AI to support these interactions
if we do not effectively leverage AI… we could lose guests
The rapid rise of artificial intelligence ('AI') enabled shopping tools may reduce our control over consumer decision making and brand loyalty
adversarial manipulation of AI models, unintended data exposure through AI systems, or reliance on AI outputs that may be inaccurate or biased
Emerging laws and regulations governing AI, including the European Union AI Act, China's generative AI regulations… may impose new compliance obligations, restrict certain uses of AI technology
audit committee… responsible for overseeing… risks related to information security, cybersecurity, data protection, and AI… cybersecurity sub-committee… comprised of our Chief AI Technology Officer

limitations: ai_investment_intensity: No quantified AI spend, headcount, or capex figures appear in the excerpts; score 3 reflects named AI roles (CATO) and named infrastructure concerns but no dollar or capacity figures.

ai_narrative_concreteness: AI use cases are framed almost entirely as risks or aspirational obligations rather than deployed initiatives with operating detail; score 2 reflects aspirational/risk framing without named production deployments.
capex_mentions: No dollar or quantity figures tied to AI/compute investment found in the excerpts.
Excerpts are risk-factor and governance sections only; business/MD&A sections describing actual AI deployments or investment budgets may exist in the full filing but are not present here.
